sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) is significant bleeding after birth, commonly defined as blood loss greater than 500 mL after vaginal birth or any blood loss that causes haemodynamic compromise. It may occur in the first 24 hours or up to 6 weeks postpartum and is a major obstetric emergency.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Heavy vaginal bleeding or passage of large clots
- Lower abdominal or pelvic pain
- Tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, dizziness, or shock
- Fever or offensive lochia in secondary PPH with infection
- Consider the 4 Ts: Tone, Tissue, Trauma, Thrombin

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Blood loss is often underestimated
- Pregnant and postpartum patients can compensate for major blood loss before showing obvious shock signs
- If the uterus is difficult to locate or feels soft, uterine atony may be present
- Retained products, infection, trauma, and coagulopathy are important causes

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Continuous support and reassurance
- Keep the patient warm and position appropriately
- Perform frequent vital signs and reassessment
- Consider oxygen if indicated
- Encourage urgent transport and early obstetric or retrieval escalation
- If the uterus is soft and uterine atony is suspected, encourage uterine massage only if within training and local protocol
- Collect clots or tissue for hospital evaluation if practical
- Be alert to concealed blood loss and rapid deterioration

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG
- Gain IV access, ideally two large-bore cannulas where possible
- Begin fluid resuscitation while expediting transfer and definitive care
- Consider point-of-care haemoglobin if available
- Consider tranexamic acid (TXA) early for active haemorrhage - administer within 3 hours of birth; do not withhold pending consultation if haemorrhage is significant and ongoing
- Consider uterotonic therapy (oxytocin) within local protocol, scope, and senior direction
- Consider antibiotic therapy only in consultation with the on-call doctor when secondary PPH or infection is suspected
- Monitor closely and record observations frequently

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- The main management targets are tone, tissue, trauma, and thrombin
- Escalate early to obstetric and retrieval support for any significant haemorrhage or shock
- Blood loss is commonly underestimated, and haemodynamic compromise may appear late
